EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low over Wyoming resulted in a late Spring Winter storm across the Beartooth/Absaroka Mountains and adjacent foothills, as well as the Northeast Big Horn Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tie Creek, Sucker Creek and Big Goose SNOTELS received 13, 17, and 16 inches of snow respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure crossed Wyoming and brought heavy snow and strong wind to much of the area. The heaviest snow fell in the favored northerly upslope areas, including Casper and Lander where anywhere from 8 to 12 inches of snow was recorded. Close to a foot of snow also fell near Green River and Rock Springs. Over a foot fell in some of the adjacent mountains as well. The snow also combined with winds that gusted over 50 mph at times that lowered visibility to near zero at times. This led to the closure of many roads for several hours, including Interstates 80 and 25.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell on Casper Mountain. Some amounts included 17 and 14 inches at the Reno Hill and Casper Mountains SNOTEL sites, respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving system moved through Wyoming. Snowfall amounts were light to moderate for the most part. However, convective banding developed over Teton County and brought locally heavy amounts of snow. The highest amount was 22 inches at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort. In the lower elevations, Teton Village had 10 new inches of snow. Amounts dropped sharply further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in portions of the Jackson Valley. The heaviest was near the Jackson Hole Ski Resort where 10 inches of snow fell near Teton Village.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Alberta Clipper moved southeast across Wyoming. Most areas only had light amounts of snow. However, local banding developed near Lander where some areas had over 6 inches of snow. More areas saw high wind as the cold front moved through. Some of the more notable wind gusts included 87 mph along the Chief Joseph Highway and 71 mph south of Casper.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred in portions of the Absarokas. Along the Chief Joseph Highway, there were several hurricane force wind gusts, including a maximum gust of 87 mph.

The heaviest snow fell in the favored northerly upslope areas, including Casper and Lander where anywhere from 8 to 12 inches of snow was recorded. Close to a foot of snow also fell near Green River and Rock Springs. Over a foot fell in some of the adjacent mountains as well. The snow also combined with winds that gusted over 50 mph at times that lowered visibility to near zero at times. This led to the closure of many roads for several hours, including Interstates 80 and 25.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of snow and wind brought sharply reduced visibility that led to the closure of US Highway 287 for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent area of low pressure developed in the Lee of the Colorado Rockies and brought heavy snow and strong wind to portions of western and central Wyoming. The hardest hit areas were Johnson, Natrona and Sweetwater Counties. The heaviest snow fell in Natrona County. Portions of Casper Mountain received over 2 feet of snow with the city of Casper getting anywhere from 9 to 18 inches. Across Sweetwater County, 6 to 10 inches of snow was common. The snow combined with winds gusting over 50 mph to bring sharply reduced visibility and the closure of many roads, including Interstates 25 and 80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in portions of southeastern Johnson County. The highest amount measured was 6 inches near Linch. The snow combined with winds gusting past 50 mph at times to bring near whiteout conditions and cause the closure of Interstate 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving system brought locally heavy snow to portions of central and southern Wyoming. The highest amounts fell in the Lander Foothills and the adjacent East Slope of the Wind River Range. Across the Lander Foothills anywhere from 10 to 20 inches of snow fell. The highest amount in the Wind River Range was 19 inches at the Townsend Creek SNOTEL site. Some other amounts included 7 inches at the Buckboard Marina near Rock Springs and 6 to 9 inches around Beaver Rim.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A man and a 67 year old woman were clearing a large amount of snow off of the roof of a house on Casper Mountain. A large amount of snow fell of the roof and buried the woman under several feet of snow. It took around 30 minutes to rescue the woman. She received treatment at the scene and was then transported to Wyoming Medical Center where she died from her injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 27 year old Wisconsin man was riding a snowmobile in down a steep slope near Togwotee Pass when he triggered a large slab avalanche around 3 pm. The man was wearing an avalanche airbag but was buried by 2 to 4 feet of snow. Three of his companions dug him out of the snow but it was 5 minutes before they could start CPR. He was pronounced dead at the scene around 5 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of Pacific fronts and troughs moved through western Wyoming and brought prolonged periods of snow with several breaks between the systems. Over the 5 days, there were rather impressive snow totals. The highest amounts fell in Teton County, where a favorable jet dynamics let to very heavy snowfall rates within convective snow bands. Over 40 inches of snow fell at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort with 10 to 20 inches common across the lower elevations. Maximum snowfall amounts around 2 feet also fell in Yellowstone Park, the Wind River Range and the Salt and Wyoming Ranges. Over a foot of snow also fell in the Star Valley. The heavy snow also led to avalanches that closed mountains passes throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell through much of Yellowstone Park. Some of the highest snowfall amounts included 24 inches at Lewis Lake Divide and 22 inches at the Old Faithful Ranger Station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well deepening Colorado Low underwent bombogenesis producing widespread heavy snow and blizzard conditions across all of southeastern Wyoming. Conditions initially began as rain on the morning of the 13th with thunderstorms across the far southeastern portion before transitioning over to snow later in the morning. By noon of the 13th, almost all of southeastern Wyoming was under blizzard conditions with Interstates 80 and 25 closed to traffic and communities issuing no travel advised to keep folks off the road. Snow foot drifts of over 10 feet tall were reported with heavy snowfall of over a foot measured. Initial estimates placed livestock losses at 4,100 cattle, 200 sheep and 40 horses statewide from snow and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Conditions measured at the Torrington Airport with wind gusts up to 48 mph including a peak wind measurement of of 52 mph at 6:53 PM MDT on the 13th. Widespread snow totals of 6.5 inches up to 15 inches across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well deepening Colorado Low underwent bombogenesis producing widespread heavy snow and blizzard conditions across all of southeastern Wyoming. Conditions initially began as rain on the morning of the 13th with thunderstorms across the far southeastern portion before transitioning over to snow later in the morning. By noon of the 13th, almost all of southeastern Wyoming was under blizzard conditions with Interstates 80 and 25 closed to traffic and communities issuing no travel advised to keep folks off the road. Snow foot drifts of over 10 feet tall were reported with heavy snowfall of over a foot measured. Initial estimates placed livestock losses at 4,100 cattle, 200 sheep and 40 horses statewide from snow and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several Wyoming Department of Transportation sensors along Interstate 80 measured periods of blizzard conditions with several sensors recording wind gusts even up to 48 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As low level moisture advection in the region coupled with frontogenesis and strong jet flow aloft, snow bands formed across the region producing areas of heavy snow. The mountains of the Snowy Range and Sierra Madre Range measured over a foot of snow above 9000 feet with areas across Goshen and Niobrara Counties with over six inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Medicine Bow sensor at elevation of 10,500 feet measured 1.90 inches of snow-water equivalent. Using a snow-to-liquid ratio of ten to one, estimated snowfall calculated to be 19.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow melt caused by warming temperatures led to minor flooding along the Middle Fork of the Popo Agie River in Lander and portions of the Wind and Little Wind Rivers. The flooding was minor and restricted to low lying areas near the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the Little Wind River around Fort Washakie, peaking at 5.1 feet. The flooding remained in low lying areas and caused no damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm become severe in eastern Sweetwater County and brought high winds. At Bitter Creek, a maximum wind gust of 76 mph was recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The weather sensor at Bitter Creek along Interstate 80 had a wind gust to 76 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough axis extending from western Wyoming south and west to the Four Corners with an associated low center and cold front passed over the forecast region creting a tight pressure gradient and strong subsidence in its wake. Most of the wind prone area across southeastern Wyoming experienced wind gusts reaching into the low 60 mph range. High peaks across the Sierra Madre and Snowy Range received over a foot of snow as part of the frontal passage over the area. The eastern portion of Laramie County had a heavy snowband move across the Albin area dropping up to six inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wyoming Department of Transportation sensor at Lone Tree along Interstate 80 or two miles west of Buford measured the reported wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strained earthen dam became breached water near the Fiddle Back Ranch releasing water as high as eight feet deep. The water eventually reached the Cheyenne River increasing the flow and level to top of the bank in places.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wyoming Highway Patrol along with the ASOS at Rawlins reported blizzard conditions during the event. A fatal crash occurred at milepost 219 on Interstate 80 around 9:37 AM. A Semi with two trailers crashed into a previous crash of two tractor trailers. The one driver involved with the first collision was outside of his vehicle when the second collision occurred. The driver then became trapped between his trailer and tractor portion of the other vehicle. He succumbed to his injuries at the scene. No other injuries were reported but the driver of the third semi was likely driving too fast for the winter road conditions, which were slick with ice and blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two middle and upper level troughs moved across the region with an associated cold front and jet entrance regions providing enough lift and moisture to produce widespread snow and low visibility across the region. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong winter storm moved across the region, bringing heavy snow and gusty winds to portions of northeastern Wyoming. Snow developed during the morning hours. Northwest winds increased during the late morning and afternoon, with areas of blowing snow developing. Snow ended late at night, with winds gradually tapering off during the morning. The heaviest snowfall was across portions of Campbell County to the Wyoming Black Hills, where six to around 12 inches were reported, while less than an inch of snow was reported in Newcastle. Blowing and drifting snow made travel very difficult across much of the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving system brought snow to the southern sections of northeastern Wyoming during the overnight hours. Snowfall of three to five inches were reported across much of Weston County.
Minor flooding occurred along the Belle Fourche River in parts of Crook County due to ice jams and snowmelt.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water flowing into and from Keyhole Reservoir in Crook County, combined with ice in the river channel, caused minor flooding along the Belle Fourche River around Moorcroft and Hulett. The reservoir elevation at Keyhole Reservoir set a record of 4101.98 feet on March 28, surpassing the previous record of 4100.38 feet set in May of 1978. The river gage on the Belle Fourche River near Alva crested at 6.28 feet, which is 1.28 feet above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved across the region, bringing areas of moderate snow and gusty winds. The highest snowfall totals were across northern Campbell County into northern Crook County, where three to six inches of snow were reported in some areas. Gusty winds in northern Crook County produced blowing and drifting snow Saturday morning.

A warm and moist airmass was situated across the Billings Forecast Area. At the same time, a cold front and upper level disturbance moved across south central and southeast Montana and north central Wyoming. As a result of the above, thunderstorms developed across many locations with a severe thunderstorm just north of Sheridan.

A surface front across the mountains and eroding stratus deck across southeastern Wyoming gave enough convective energy coupled with favorable mid-level lapse rates and surface heating to produce severe hail and a tornado across Laramie County. Tornado reported did not produce any visible damage or escarpment of the soil to determine an appropriate track, width, and scale designation. Hail over 1 in size reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports described portions of Interstate 80 between Cheyenne and Hillsdale covered by the hail making for difficult travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front across the mountains and eroding stratus deck across southeastern Wyoming gave enough convective energy coupled with favorable mid-level lapse rates and surface heating to produce severe hail and a tornado across Laramie County. Tornado reported did not produce any visible damage or escarpment of the soil to determine an appropriate track, width, and scale designation. Hail over 1 in size reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer noticed brief touchdown of tornado from residence with no visible damage produced. Coordinating with local law enforcement and emergency management, no damage was confirmed with no discernible soil and grass disturbance to effectively determine path length and width and should be considered a rough estimate.

Gusty winds produced some blowing and drifting snow.

A storm system moved out of the central Rockies and into the Plains states and brought heavy snow to portions of central Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell along the eastern slopes of the Wind River Range where over a foot of new snow was common with a maximum of 27 inches at the Townsend Creek SNOTEL site. In the lower elevations, the heaviest snow was in the Lander Foothills where amounts ranged from 7.7 inches at the airport to 19 inches near Sinks Canyon State Park. Other hard hit areas included northern Johnson County and the Green Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Lander Aiport, 7.7 inches of snow was measured. Around the region, amounts ranged from seven inches in Lander to 18 inches in Sinks Canyon State Park.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Home of observer was damaged by hail with several windows broken along with unspecified damage to vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front across the mountains and eroding stratus deck across southeastern Wyoming gave enough convective energy coupled with favorable mid-level lapse rates and surface heating to produce severe hail and a tornado across Laramie County. Tornado reported did not produce any visible damage or escarpment of the soil to determine an appropriate track, width, and scale designation. Hail over 1 in size reported.

An avalanche closed US 189 / 191 for around 3 hours on the afternoon of April 4th. At 1:00 pm, crews responded to an avalanche that reached the roadway. The crew also performed some avalanche mitigation work to increase safety. The roadway was then opened again at 3:45 pm.

A weak landspout tornado touched down briefly in Marbleton and traveled for around a tenth of a mile before lifting. A portion of a roof was blown off a manufactured home, a travel trailer was destroyed and another mobile home was moderately damaged.  The tornado was rated an EF0 with maximum winds of 80 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landspout tornado touched down in Marbleton. Please see event summary for details.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved out of the central Rockies and into the Plains states and brought heavy snow to portions of central Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell along the eastern slopes of the Wind River Range where over a foot of new snow was common with a maximum of 27 inches at the Townsend Creek SNOTEL site. In the lower elevations, the heaviest snow was in the Lander Foothills where amounts ranged from 7.7 inches at the airport to 19 inches near Sinks Canyon State Park. Other hard hit areas included northern Johnson County and the Green Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported seven inches of snow about 13 miles south-southeast of Buffalo.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty National Weather Service meteorologist reported penny size hail in Kaycee.

A couple of Pacific fronts moved through western Wyoming and brought heavy snow to many locations. The heaviest snow fell in the Tetons where totals of over two feet were common. The highest amount was 44 inches at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort. Many areas in the Jackson Valley received over a foot of snow. Amounts of over two feet of new snow also occurred in the Salt and Wyoming Range and Yellowstone Park. At Bondurant, 26 inches of new snow fell. The heavy snow led to numerous avalanches that shut down many roads for a time. Meanwhile, high winds occurred across portions of Park, Natrona and Fremont Counties. A mountain wave developed near Clark in Park County and a maximum wind gust of 114 mph was measured. Hurricane force wind gusts occurred in the Green Mountains. Winds gusts over 60 mph occurred in the vicinity of Casper. These high winds over the Wind River Range resulted in the closure of South Pass for a time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds occurred in portions of the Green Mountains. The strongest winds were at the highest elevations where wind gusts of 89 and 74 mph were recorded at the Fales Rock and Camp Creek RAWS sites, respectively. At Beaver Rim along Highway 28, a maximum wind gust of 76 mph was recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of Pacific fronts and troughs brought another long lived snow event to western Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell in the Tetons and adjacent Jackson Valley. Snowfall amounts in the Tetons over 30 inches were common. The highest amount was 63 inches of new snow at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort. Snowfall amounts in the Jackson Valley generally ranged from 12 to 30 inches. Over three feet of new snow fell in Yellowstone Park with over two feet in portions of the Absarokas. The heavy snow led to numerous avalanches that closed passes and roads around the Jackson Valley. Strong winds occurred along the eastern slopes of the mountains, including a 60 mph at Sinks Canyon State Park near Lander.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell throughout much of Yellowstone Park. Some of the highest amounts included 39 inches at the Two Oceans Plateau SNOTEL and 36 inches at the Lewis Lake SNOTEL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of Pacific fronts moved through western Wyoming and brought heavy snow to many locations. The heaviest snow fell in the Tetons where totals of over two feet were common. The highest amount was 44 inches at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort. Many areas in the Jackson Valley received over a foot of snow. Amounts of over two feet of new snow also occurred in the Salt and Wyoming Range and Yellowstone Park. At Bondurant, 26 inches of new snow fell. The heavy snow led to numerous avalanches that shut down many roads for a time. Meanwhile, high winds occurred across portions of Park, Natrona and Fremont Counties. A mountain wave developed near Clark in Park County and a maximum wind gust of 114 mph was measured. Hurricane force wind gusts occurred in the Green Mountains. Winds gusts over 60 mph occurred in the vicinity of Casper. These high winds over the Wind River Range resulted in the closure of South Pass for a time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Much of Yellowstone Park received anywhere from 15 to 30 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level trough interacted with a surface arctic front across the region as frontogenetic forcing aided in the production of snow and strong winds. Much of Carbon County received heavy snow not just in the Snowy or Sierra Madre Ranges with impacts of the heavy snow and winds on area interstates and highways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer in Baggs measured 7.5 inches of snowfall and reported it to use via Facebook.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Old Battle sensor at elevation of 10,000 feet measured a snow water equivalent of 0.70 inches and using a snow to liquid ratio of 20 to 1 estimated total snowfall at 14 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Site located one mile north of Rawlins measured 6.8 inches of snowfall while another public report two miles west-northwest of Rawlins measured 7.0 inches of snowfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wyoming Department of Transportation closed Highways 287 and 789 between Rawlins and mile marker 23 by Muddy Gap due to winter conditions. Strong blowing snow coupled with heavy snowfall across the region kept the highway closed for over 12 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of Pacific fronts and troughs moved through western Wyoming and brought prolonged periods of snow with several breaks between the systems. Over the 5 days, there were rather impressive snow totals. The highest amounts fell in Teton County, where a favorable jet dynamics let to very heavy snowfall rates within convective snow bands. Over 40 inches of snow fell at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort with 10 to 20 inches common across the lower elevations. Maximum snowfall amounts around 2 feet also fell in Yellowstone Park, the Wind River Range and the Salt and Wyoming Ranges. Over a foot of snow also fell in the Star Valley. The heavy snow also led to avalanches that closed mountains passes throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy snow fell over much of the Tetons. The heaviest snow fell at the Jackson Hole Resort where 49 inches of new snow was measured. Numerous SNOTEL sites have 2 to 3 feet of new snow over the duration of the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought periods of snow to the area over a 24 hour period. Most areas saw two to four inches of fluffy snowfall. However, heavier upslope snowfall fell in the Newcastle area, where close to a foot fell. Wind chill values were well below zero in many areas.

In addition, a strong thunderstorm produced several inches of accumulating pea and marble hail along Interstate 80 near Laramie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Active flow pattern over the first few days of the month produced widespread severe storms across southeastern Wyoming with strong winds from downdrafts in western Carbon County to hail across the high plains. Of note, a cold air funnel developed on the fourth prompting widespread reports and attention. As such with the development of these funnels, the funnel lifted back up never reaching the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind measured by the sensor at the Dixon Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm become severe as it moved into the Bighorn Mountains. A tornado touched down near Powder River Pass and moved eastward, initially very close to US 16 and continuing through the mountains in Johnson County. Numerous trees were uprooted and snapped along its path. In addition, several snow fences were torn apart and thrown in all directions. Peak winds were estimated at 110 mph, making the tornado an EF-1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched down in the Big Horn Mountains near Powder River Pass and traveled east. Please see event summary for details.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of heavy rainfall over the Big Horn Mountains and adjacent foothills resulted in widespread flooding and major impacts along the foothills of Sheridan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several days of heavy rainfall over the Big Horn Mountains and adjacent foothills, as well as the lower elevations resulted in widespread flooding and major impacts along the foothills of Sheridan County. The State Engineers Office Water Division Office measured up to 4 inches of rain between May 26th and May 28th. The following are some of the multiple impacts that occurred across the area:|-Sheridan County Commissioners issued an Emergency Declaration.|-  It was reported that Goose Creek saw stream levels rise 3 feet in 15 minutes in Kendrick Subdivision on the 28th. |-The Connor Battlefield State Historic site in Ranchester estimated cost of the damage to roads in that area of $25,000.|-Ranchester City Park was closed due to the flooding. |-Jackson Creek overtopped the road in the town of Big Horn. |-Water covered portions of Tongue Canyon Road. |-Flooding on the Little Goose resulted in the following road closures:|  -Soldier Creek road 11 miles west of Sheridan|  -Woodland Park Road south of Sheridan|  -67 by Wolf Creek in Ranchester|  -County Road 116 near Smith Creek.|-One observer reported it was the worse flooding they had seen since 1978. |-The Woodland Park Village (a trailer park of 250 homes) was severely impacted by the |  flooding. Other homes in the area were under water.||On May 30th, waters receded below flood stage on all of the gauges in Sheridan County. Local emergency officials did not report any additional flooding concerns. However, some roads remained closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Across Johnson County, two to four inches of rain fell from May 27th through May 28th. This caused rapid rises of creeks and streams as well as the Middle Fork of the Powder River. The river gauge at 10 miles upstream of Kaycee peaked at 10.3 feet, which is 2.3 feet above flood stage. In Kaycee, minor flooding occurred, especially around the fairgrounds but no damage was reported. Moderate flooding also occurred downstream of Barnum, but no damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NCRS reported the Middle Fork of the Powder River out of its banks in a few locations around Kaycee. The flooding was minor and no damage was reported.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL LARAMIE RANGE AND SOUTHWEST PLATTE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds were felt the evening of the 30th into the afternoon hours of Halloween across the wind prone areas of southeast Wyoming. Several sites along the interstates measured wind gusts above 58 mph. With the high winds, blowing snow created slick roadway conditions along Interstate 25 near Bordeaux and Wheatland where a commercial truck crashed into a nearby Wyoming Highway Patrol vehicle injuring the female officer inside as she was in the process of aiding a stranded vehicle. The officer was wearing her seat belt at the time of accident and suffered minor injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wyoming Department of Transportation sensor along Interstate 25 at Bordeaux or nine miles south of Wheatland measured 58 mph gusts and periods of sustained 40 mph winds creating blowing snow across the interstate for slick road conditions. Due to slick road conditions brought on by the blowing snow, a Wyoming Highway Patrol Trooper was injured when a commercial truck hit her patrol car while she was assisting a stranded motorist. Accident occurred around milepost 66 on Interstate 25 south of Wheatland. The trooper was wearing her seatbelt at the time of accident and treated for minor injuries. The accident and subsequent clean up closed the northbound lanes of interstate for about three and a half hours.

Multiple spotters were tracking a supercell thunderstorm near the Wyoming and South Dakota border along the Weston and Niobrara County line on the 3rd. The rotating thunderstorm produced a visible funnel cloud that over the course of seventeen minutes lifted and dropped off the ground producing possible several tornado touchdown points. Given no reports of any structural damage or distinctive elements for a quality survey, tornado rating will remain unknown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorm produced rotating wall cloud and associated funnel cloud that over the course of seventeen minutes produced multiple touchdown points as relayed from the Rapid City NWS office. Given the lack of damage reports and ground disturbance, no survey was formally conducted leaving the rating and relevant stats as unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Fishhawk Fire was started by lightning and was discovered on September 2nd around 40 miles west of Cody. A combination of hot, dry weather, low humidity and gusty winds allowed for rapid spread of the fire through the 5th of September. The fire led to numerous closures of trails. Some mandatory evacuations were also issued, including for the Buffalo Bill Boy Scout camp. Cooler temperatures and rain began slow the spread of the fire around the 7th of September. The fire was 100% contained by the end of the month. In total, 11,171 acres was burned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early season winter storm moved across southeastern Wyoming producing the first snowfall for the 2019-2020 season. Heavy snow was measured across portions of Niobrara County and the North Snowy Range Foothills with reports of 6 and 7 inches of snow measured respectively. Slick road conditions across Interstate 80 near Arlington contributed to a vehicle crash that closed the eastbound lanes several hours for clean up. No known injuries associated with the crash.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer located roughly two miles south of Elk Mountain measured a total snowfall of 7.0. In addition, Interstate 80 through this stretch was slick due to snowfall contributing to a vehicle crash closing the eastbound lanes for a period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system and frontal boundary moved across the region producing widespread high winds across southeastern Wyoming with light snowfall while in Carbon County blizzard conditions were reported by Arlington stopping traffic for a period of time. Heavy snow was reported in the mountain ranges in the area with close to two feet in the highest spots of the Snowy Range. A hunter out in the Sierra Madre Range went missing during the storm and his body has yet to be recovered.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The HADS sensor at the Seminoe Dam measured the reported wind gust.

A hunter out in the Sierra Madre Range went missing during the storm and his body has yet to be recovered.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wyoming Department of Transportation sensor at Bordeaux  or nine miles south of Wheatland along Interstate 25 measured the reported wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flash freeze winter event started the evening of the 26th through the morning and early afternoon hours on the 27th. Initial rain and freezing rain created slick conditions that iced up when precipitation transitioned to snow. The poor road conditions contributed to several accidents on area interstates, one of which contributed to a fatal crash north of Cheyenne. Highest snowfall totals were over a foot of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snowfall accumulation of 1.5 to near 3 inches were reported across the forecast zone with much of the snowfall falling during the overnight and morning hours. A driver heading southbound along Interstate 25 near mile marker 32.5 rolled their pickup truck twice after losing control due to falling snow and icy road conditions. The individual was transported to area hospital where they died from their injuries. The driver was wearing their seatbelt at the time of the crash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure brought locally heavy snow to western Wyoming. In the Tetons, over a foot of new snow fell at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort. In northwest flow behind the associated cold front, strong winds occurred. The strongest winds were in Johnson County with a maximum gust of 74 mph at the Buffalo airport as well as Red Canyon along Highway 28 in Fremont County with a maximum wind gust of 76 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Jackson Hole Ski Resort, 15 inches of new snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system and frontal boundary moved across the region producing widespread high winds across southeastern Wyoming with light snowfall while in Carbon County blizzard conditions were reported by Arlington stopping traffic for a period of time. Heavy snow was reported in the mountain ranges in the area with close to two feet in the highest spots of the Snowy Range. A hunter out in the Sierra Madre Range went missing during the storm and his body has yet to be recovered.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow accumulations were estimated by several area sensors including the Blackhall Mountain with 16.5 inches of snowfall and the Webber Springs with 12.0 inches. A huntsman went missing during the snowstorm prompting area search and rescue, cadaver dogs and civil air patrols in hopes of finding and recovering the individual. After a week of searching, the Carbon County Sheriff's Office suspended the operations. Given that the individual has not been recovered, a proper autopsy to determine likely cause of death remains unknown though given the winter elements of wind, snow and subsequent freezing temperatures, assumption will be made to declare the fatality as direct.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front dropped southward across Wyoming and brought in an unusually cold air mass. The result was many records were broken. The locations that saw monthly record low temperatures broken included Casper, Riverton and Lander, Wyoming. The coldest temperatures occurred in the morning of October 30th. The most impressive was at Big Piney, Wyoming, where a combination of a clear sky, fresh snow cover and light winds resulted in a low of minus 26, which broke the record by 29 degrees. Note: All low temperatures in events occurred in the morning of October 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many locations in northeastern Johnson County fell below zero, including minus 15 to the southeast of Story, Wyoming. At the Buffalo, Wyoming airport, a low of three degrees below zero was recorded, setting a new all time record low for the month of October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two tornadoes formed from a supercell thunderstorm moving off of the Laramie Range in southeastern Wyoming. Both tornadoes remained over open field with no discernible damage reported from NWS Storm Survey to assign any other rating but unknown. Severe thunderstorms in the area as well produced large hail up to softballs that created extensive damage in and around the Glendo area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moved over the top of a ridge and brought severe thunderstorms to portions of northern and central Wyoming. The strongest storm moved through Hot Springs County, where a supercell thunderstorm brought hail over two inches in diameter, including to the city of Thermopolis, Wyoming. There were numerous reports of broken windows on homes and cars as well as damaged siding in the city of Thermopolis.  Another storm brought quarter sized hail northwest of Riverton. Another thunderstorm rapidly developed over Natrona County, southwest of Casper and brought golf ball sized hail along Highway 220.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter size hail fell from near Kinnear to north of Riverton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm moved into Natrona County and rapidly strengthened as it moved toward Highway 220 southwest of Casper. The thunderstorm peaked in intensity along Highway 220. A motorist reported golf ball size hail when driving through the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm developed over Hot Springs and Park County and peaked in intensity over Washakie County. There were reports of nickel sized hail near Worland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported nickel sized hail near Worland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front across Wyoming served as the focus for another round of severe thunderstorms across central Wyoming. The strongest storm developed over the lee of the Bighorn Mountains and become severe as it moved into the southern portion of the county. Up to golf ball size hail fell near Sussex. Another strong storm brought penny size hail near Thermopolis in Hot Springs County. At the Jackson Hole airport, a man was struck by lightning in the parking lot.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm dropped penny sized hail in the vicinity of Barnum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms developed over portions of northern  Wyoming. One thunderstorm caused roof and tree damage in Worland and downed tree limbs near Ten Sleep. This storm then moved northeastward and brought a wind gust of 68 mph to the Greybull airport. Another storm brought half dollar sized hail to northeastern Johnson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported half dollar sized hail, 21 miles northeast of Buffalo, Wyoming.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high based thunderstorm collapsed near Worland, Wyoming and produced a wind gust of 65 mph at the Worland airport. In Hot Springs County a high based shower collapsed and produced a 67 mph wind gust in Hot Springs County, Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a 67 mph when a high based shower collapsed over the spotters house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high based thunderstorm collapsed near Worland, Wyoming and produced a wind gust of 65 mph at the Worland airport. In Hot Springs County a high based shower collapsed and produced a 67 mph wind gust in Hot Springs County, Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Worland, Wyoming airport reported a wind gust of 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms brought an average of three quarters of an inch of  rain in only 20 minutes along with large amounts of small hail on the late afternoon of July 14th. There were many instances of street flooding around Powell, Wyoming with many businesses, houses and the animal shelter flooded. Local water cleanup companies reported 50 calls for water cleanup, especially for flooded basements.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly an inch of rain and a large amount of large hail fell in only 20 minutes around Powell, Wyoming. The result were numerous areas that were flooded, including streets, businesses and homes. The main damage was from water in basements and flooded roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moving southeastward off of the Absarokas brought another round of large hail around Thermopolis, Wyoming. There were numerous reports of hail from quarter to ping pong ball size along the path of the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of quarter to ping pong ball sized hail from west of Thermopolis to Thermopolis to Hot Springs State Park in Hot Springs County, Wyoming.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across portions of Goshen County producing widespread rain and accumulating hail. The combination of hail and rain led to increased flooding across ranch roads in addition to shutting down United State Highway 85 due to water flowing over the roadway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rushing water was flowing over the road at the intersection of Road 70 and Road 19.

An arctic front dropped southward across Wyoming and brought in an unusually cold air mass. The result was many records were broken. The locations that saw monthly record low temperatures broken included Casper, Riverton and Lander, Wyoming. The coldest temperatures occurred in the morning of October 30th. The most impressive was at Big Piney, Wyoming, where a combination of a clear sky, fresh snow cover and light winds resulted in a low of minus 26, which broke the record by 29 degrees. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very cold temperatures occurred in Yellowstone Park. Some of the coldest temperatures included minus 25 at the Old Faithful Ranger Station and minus 24 at the northeast Entrance. These low temperatures occurred on the morning of October 30th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm first developed over far-east central Albany County near 3:45pm MDT. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm first developed over far-east central Albany County near 3:45pm MDT. This storm moved east to east-southeast into Platte County near 4:15pm MDT with increasing signs of low-level rotation per KCYS radar. The storm continued east-southeast with weak low-level rotation and supportive reflectivity structure. A second Tornado Warning was issued at 4:57pm MDT. Numerous reports |of funnels from chasers and storm spotters were received just after 5pm MDT. Much stronger low-level rotation developed near 5:14pm MDT via KCYS radar with confirmed tornado touchdown occurring at 5:15pm MDT. The touchdown occurred 3 miles west of |Interstate 25 west of mile marker 49. The cone tornado was observed on the ground via live-stream, pictures, and video through numerous social media channels. Tornado remained west of Interstate 25 through the entirety of its life cycle as it roped out about 1 mile west of the interstate over open ranch land. Time of dissipation is near 5:30pm MDT. Despite extensive investigation along county roads, no damage targets|were able to be identified in the open ranch land and thus an unknown EF rating was assigned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm developed during the late afternoon and produced an EF-1 tornado near Bosler, Wyoming and while it moved over the Laramie Range into the high plains of southeastern Wyoming produced another shortlived tornado over the open range rendering an EF-Unknown rating. In addition, numerous hail reports from this and other thunderstorms dropped up to golf ball size stones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm developed near the town of Rock River, Wyoming, in west-central Albany County.  At 606 PM MDT, the storm rapidly intensified as it moved southeast.  A tornado touched down and crossed Highway 30 south of Bosler, Wyoming at 610 PM MDT, and dissipated at 619 PM MDT.  The storm continued to show signs of rotation as it moved east-southeast toward the Laramie County line, however no additional tornadoes were reported.||A survey team from the National Weather Service in Cheyenne met with officials from Albany County to investigate storm damage in the Bosler area.  Eyewitness reports and snapped wooden power poles along County Road 51 indicated the initial tornado touched down in an open field about 2.5 miles west-southwest of Bosler, or 18.5 miles north-northwest of Laramie.  The tornado lifted for a short time but descended again and caused complete destruction of a pole barn near Highway 30 about three miles south-southeast of Bosler.  The tornado crossed Highway 30, confirmed by aerial photographs provided by the City of Laramie.  Large pieces of warped metal were blown several hundred yards to the east of the highway in a wide swath.  The damage footprint ended near the point of the original report.  Damage was consistent with an EF-1 tornado with maximum winds around 100 mph.

In addition, numerous hail reports from this and other thunderstorms dropped up to golf ball size stones.

A severe thunderstorm formed in the Big Horn Basin. A 63 mph wind gust was reported at the Greybull, Wyomin airport when an outflow boundary from the storm moved through. In Yellowstone Park, a lightning strike on Mount Holmes set the historic fire lookout on fire. The building was a total loss. A radio repeater was also damaged by the lightning strike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike set the historic Mount Holmes fire lookout on fire, resulting in a total loss. The lightning strike also damaged a radio repeater on the mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were a pair of severe thunderstorms on this day. One storm dropped through Johnson County, Wyoming. Wind gusts to 68 mph were reported along Interstate 25 near Kaycee, Wyoming. Near Jackson, Wyoming, a microburst downed trees at the Colter Elementary School, with one causing minor roof damage. To the east of DuBois, Wyoming, heavy rain brought a second day of flooding over US 20 26, closing the road again and caused damage to a property.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second day of slow moving thunderstorms brought a second day of mud and water flowing across US 20 26 southeast of Dubois. The Wyoming Department of Transportation was once again called out to clear mud and debris from the road. A nearby resident had two culverts damaged on his property as well as roads and landscaping.

With a stationary boundary remaining over the area, there were two rounds of severe weather across central Wyoming on Independence Day. One storm developed in the lee of the Wind River Range and strengthened as it moved eastward toward Natrona County, Wyoming. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported north of Casper, Wyoming. A second round of thunderstorms developed in the afternoon and evening hours. There were more reports of large hail, the largest being ping pong ball sized in Evansville. Strong wind gusts up to 65 mph were also recorded at a few locations. Heavy rain caused the wash out of a BLM road in Hot Springs County, Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported ping pong ball sized hail north of Casper, Wyoming along Interstate 25 near the Highway 259 exit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front dropped southward across Wyoming and brought in an unusually cold air mass. The result was many records were broken. The locations that saw monthly record low temperatures broken included Casper, Riverton and Lander, Wyoming. The coldest temperatures occurred in the morning of October 30th. The most impressive was at Big Piney, Wyoming, where a combination of a clear sky, fresh snow cover and light winds resulted in a low of minus 26, which broke the record by 29 degrees. Note: All low temperatures in events occurred in the morning of October 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record cold temperatures occurred at the Greybull airport three nights in a row. The coldest temperature occurred on October 30th with a temperature of three degrees occurring. This record low temperature broke set an all time low for the month, which was nine degrees set in 2006.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning-caused wildfire started in August and continuing into September burned over twenty-three thousand acres in addition to destroying seven buildings. The buildings included outbuildings and permanent or seasonal homes. Several days of extreme fire growth coincided with red flag warnings along with area evacuations of residents. The fire was officially contained in early September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning-caused wildfire started in August and continuing into September burned over twenty-three thousand acres in addition to destroying seven buildings. The buildings included outbuildings and permanent or seasonal homes. Several days of extreme fire growth coincided with red flag warnings along with area evacuations of residents. The fire was officially contained in early September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moved over the top of a ridge and brought severe thunderstorms to portions of northern and central Wyoming. The strongest storm moved through Hot Springs County, where a supercell thunderstorm brought hail over two inches in diameter, including to the city of Thermopolis, Wyoming. There were numerous reports of broken windows on homes and cars as well as damaged siding in the city of Thermopolis.  Another storm brought quarter sized hail northwest of Riverton. Another thunderstorm rapidly developed over Natrona County, southwest of Casper and brought golf ball sized hail along Highway 220.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a funnel cloud 18 miles SSE of Kaycee.

Numerous slow moving thunderstorms developed across central Wyoming and some became severe. On severe thunderstorm crossed US 20 26 in eastern Fremont County and brought ping pong ball size hail that damaged a pick up truck. Another storm produced a 62 mph near Linch in Johnson County. In Worland Wyoming, over an inch of rain and small hail fell in only 25 minutes and brought street flooding that backed into several buildings in town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 62 mph wind gust was reported near Linch, Wyoming.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed on the afternoon of August 22nd and brought local severe weather. In eastern Johnson County, the public reported quarter size hail. At the Greybull airport, an outflow boundary from distant thunderstorms brought a 65 mph wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum wind gust of 65 mph was reported at the Greybull, Wyoming airport from a outflow boundary from distant thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm passed to the north of Casper and spawned a tornado north of Bar Nunn near Antelope Hills. The tornado was viewed by many people and was well photographed. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landspout tornado touched down near Antelope Hills to the north of Bar Nunn, Wyoming. The tornado remained over open country and caused no damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning-caused wildfire intensified in the end of August burning over twenty-three thousand acres in addition to destroying four buildings. The fire continued burning into the month of September.

The fire continued burning into the month of September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms merged along an outflow boundary near the city of Cheyenne, Wyoming. Large hail and flash flooding occurred along with an outflow boundary interaction producing a short-lived EF-unknown landspout tornado that crossed Interstate 80 by Burns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Images and eye-witness reports confirmed the presence of a short-lived landspout tornado formed by thunderstorm outflow winds east of Cheyenne. The landspout tornado in its life cycle moved southeastward crossing Interstate 80 and knocking over a wind break fence. Given the lack of a damage indicator for the fence and no other damage reported, the landspout tornado is listed as an EF-unknown with path and width estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent topographic uplift flow contributed to a prolonged period of mountain snow over the Snowy and Sierra Madre Ranges. Storm total snowfall for several SNOTEL sites in both areas measured over two feet of snow. The sensor at Med Bow with an elevation of 10,510 feet measured an estimated snowfall total of 43.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Old Battle, Whiskey Park, Blackhall Mountain, and Little Snake River sites each measured total snowfall values of over two feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of Pacific moisture and an associated cold front moved across the area and brought heavy snow and strong wind to some areas. In Natrona County, a tight pressure gradient and strong low level southwesterly winds mixing to the surface brought high winds. A maximum wind gust of 76 mph was reported along Wyoming Boulevard south of Casper with 62 mph wind gusts reported at the Natrona County airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in portions of the Tetons. The highest amount measured was 16 inches at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm became severe in the Devils Tower area, producing hail around quarter size.

A bundle of Mylar Balloons were caught in power lines and caused an electrical arc that started a fire near the northern end of Jackson. Strong winds and dry fuels caused the fire to spread rapidly and led to the evacuation of the Saddle Butte subdivision. The fire was contained after around four days and eventually put out. In total 255 acres were burned.

A strong ridge of high pressure lingered over the Rockies and brought record setting heat to western and central Wyoming. The heat peaked on September 2nd, Labor Day. Three sites, Casper, Rock Springs and the Riverton airport, reached all-time September high temperatures with 98, 90 and 96 respectively. The Rock Springs record high was especially notable since it was the first time the station reached 90 degrees in September. Other record high temperatures included 98 in Greybull and Worland, 95 in Lander, 89 in Big Piney and 79 at Lake Yellowstone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Casper airport reached a record high temperature of 98 degrees. This was the all time record high temperature for September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across the southeastern Wyoming High Plains producing large hail over softball size and numerous tornadoes in Platte and Goshen Counties. The highest rating tornado was an EF-2 north of Fort Laramie that was on the ground for close to thirty minutes. Couple residential homes were damaged by the tornado in addition to several livestock and wildlife animals killed by flying debris. The supercell that produced this tornado also produced an earlier tornado in Platte County and passed into Goshen before producing the EF-2 tornado. Later, another supercell tornado produced a shortlived tornado east of Chugwater and dissipated shortly after passing into Goshen County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across the southeastern Wyoming High Plains producing large hail over softball size and numerous tornadoes in Platte and Goshen Counties. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across the southeastern Wyoming High Plains producing large hail over softball size and numerous tornadoes in Platte and Goshen Counties. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Official from Yoder observed the tornado as it moved in from Platte County over the open high plains. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across the southeastern Wyoming High Plains producing large hail over softball size and numerous tornadoes in Platte and Goshen Counties. The highest rating tornado was an EF-2 north of Fort Laramie that was on the ground for close to thirty minutes. Couple residential homes were damaged by the tornado in addition to several livestock and wildlife animals killed by flying debris. The supercell that produced this tornado also produced an earlier tornado in Platte County and passed into Goshen before producing the EF-2 tornado. Later, another supercell tornado produced a shortlived tornado east of Chugwater and dissipated shortly after passing into Goshen County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the course of the tornadic supercell thunderstorm, several storm chasers and emergency managers were reporting the sighting of a wedge-shaped tornado moving across the high plains. During the lifetime of the tornado, videos submitted to the office referenced multi-vortex tornadoes and various satellite tornadoes as it neared and eventually passed over United States Highway 85. Several buildings were damaged in the path of the twister along with various lifestock and wild animals killed by flying debris. Damage at one of the residences was equivalent to EF-2 damage. Given the sparse and remote areas that the tornado passed through, path length and width are at best an estimate given that the tornado path was discontinuous and at time impossible to investigate given private land and no road access.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front passed through western and central Wyoming, pushing out an abnormally warm air mass and bringing some high winds. The winds occurred both with showers and thunderstorms as well as gradient winds as the front passed. Some of the strongest winds included 73 mph at Lander and 71 mph to the south of Casper.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous wind gusts over 58 mph along Wyoming Boulevard to the south of Casper. The highest gust reported was 71 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wyoming Department of Transportation sensor along Interstate 80 west of Laramie at Quealy Dome measured the peak sustained winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Union Pacific Railroad sensor at Lynchwood or nine miles southeast of Buford measured the reported wind gust. Several instances of sustained winds up to 43 mph were measured in addition.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure system passed through the region in the days after Christmas dropping heavy snow across the far reaches of southeastern Wyoming. Coupled with the heavy snow was gusty winds that created many reports of drifting snow in excess of a couple feet. Highest snowfall totals were across eastern Laramie County with nine inches being the highest total north of Burns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observers across the city of Cheyenne measured various heavy snowfall totals from six to eight inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the winter storm moved through the area, conditions along Interstate 80 on the afternoon of the 20th quickly deteriorated with icy conditions and limited visibility from blowing snow. A single vehicle rollover crash occurred at 4 PM on Wednesday where a female passenger who was not wearing her seatbelt succombed to her injuries at the scene after collapsing upon exiting the crashed vehicle. The driver of the vehicle was driving too fast for the conditions as noted in the Wyoming Highway Patrol report and lost control as the vehicle exited the interstate and sliding before rolling once. Across the majority of Carbon County, heavy snowfall amounts reaching up to six inches and drifts as high as two feet were reported along with the Old Battle SNOTEL in the Sierra Madre measuring twelve inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer located in Rawlins measured a total snowfall of 6.0 inches with noted drifts of up to 2 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system tracked into the central Great Plains producing a tight pressure gradient and trowal winter precipitation that produced blizzard or near-blizzard conditions and high winds throughout portions of southeastern Wyoming. Peak wind gusts were measured into the lower 80 mph range with several drifts exceeding two feet across the region. Heavy snowfall across the Sierra Madre Range was also measured. Travelers across the area interstates came to a halt due to the hazardous conditions for them and the maintenance crews to safely clear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters across northern Goshen County near Jay Em and south-southwest of Lusk reported blizzard conditions with visibility less than 50 feet and heavy snow of over 6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system tracked into the central Great Plains producing a tight pressure gradient and trowal winter precipitation that produced blizzard or near-blizzard conditions and high winds throughout portions of southeastern Wyoming. Peak wind gusts were measured into the lower 80 mph range with several drifts exceeding two feet across the region. Heavy snowfall across the Sierra Madre Range was also measured. Travelers across the area interstates came to a halt due to the hazardous conditions for them and the maintenance crews to safely clear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sage Creek Basin site at an elevation of 7850 feet measured half inch of snow-water equivalent and the application of the a 12 to 1 ratio produced an estimated snowfall amount of 6.0 inches. Due to blowing snow and hazardous road conditions, Interstate 80 was closed to all traffic for several hours during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system tracked into the central Great Plains producing a tight pressure gradient and trowal winter precipitation that produced blizzard or near-blizzard conditions and high winds throughout portions of southeastern Wyoming. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several Wyoming Department of Transportation sensors along Interstate 80 measured and reported blizzard conditions. The interstate was closed for several hours due to the inability to quickly and efficiently remove drifting snow and the treacherous conditions that would endanger human life. Several sensors along the interstate measured high wind warning winds in the area with the strongest being at Strouss Hill at 73 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, low pressure system moved across the region in the days prior to the Thanksgiving Holiday producing widespread heavy snow and blowing snow conditions that shuttered down overall transportation for travelers and commerce along the interstates. Heaviest snowfall totals of over two feet measured at SNOTEL sites in the southern mountain ranges. The city of Cheyenne measured over a foot of snow causing enough disruption for the city and state offices there to close on Tuesday due to the poor travel conditions. A traveler in southwest Carbon County upon crashing his vehicle that subsequently burst into flames wandered off the main road was found a day later in a ravine succumbed to exposure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gentleman traveling through the area crashed his vehicle on the afternoon of the 25th during heavy snowfall in the area along state highway 70 near Dixon. The vehicle as a result of the crash caught on fire with the driver becoming lost after walking away. Crews were unable to locate the driver due to the inclement weather resulting in his body being found in a ravine a mile and a half from the crash the next morning. Coroner reports death was due to exposure from the elements with snowfall and temperatures in the 20s. Snowfall amounts on the eastern side of the forecast zone near the Sierra Madre Range were estimated in the 6 to 8 inch range with likely deep drifts.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Battle Mountain sensor at an elevation of 7440 feet measured a snow water equivalent of 0.9 inches and applying a ratio of 14 to 1 gave an estimated 12.6 inches of snowfall as there was some initial rainfall measured as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of June 30, thunderstorms with heavy rain moved through parts of northeastern Wyoming. Two to four inches of rain fell across southeast Crook and eastern Weston Counties. Runoff from the heavy rain caused flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple road culverts were overwhelmed with water and debris, causing water to flow over several county roads. Runoff from the heavy rain also caused water to run over Highway 585 southeast of Sundance.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across western and central Wyoming. Snow accompanied the cold front and amounts generally remained light to moderate. However, a combination of blowing snow and strong winds caused numerous accidents along Interstate 80 to the east of Rock Springs that resulted in the closure of the road.

An area of low pressure developed over the four corners region and moved toward Wyoming and brought an intense snowstorm to much of western and central Wyoming. In the mountains, the highest snowfall amounts fell in the Tetons where over two feet was commonplace. Casper Mountain also saw two feet of snow. Across the lower elevations, some of the more notable amounts included 10 inches at Wamsutter in Sweetwater County, 11 inches in Jackson in Teton County and 9.5 inches at Hudson in Fremont County. In addition, strong winds with some gusts past 50 mph caused blowing and drifting snow. This resulted in many road closures including Interstates 80 and 25.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were generally moderate in northern Johnson County. However, a combination of heavy snow and winds gusting as high as 60 mph brought blizzard conditions at times. Interstate 25 was shut down for several hours as a result. In addition, a period of freezing drizzle before the snow brought slick roads that led to many accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system tracked into the central Great Plains producing a tight pressure gradient and trowal winter precipitation that produced blizzard or near-blizzard conditions and high winds throughout portions of southeastern Wyoming. Peak wind gusts were measured into the lower 80 mph range with several drifts exceeding two feet across the region. Heavy snowfall across the Sierra Madre Range was also measured. Travelers across the area interstates came to a halt due to the hazardous conditions for them and the maintenance crews to safely clear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions were measured at the Douglas, Converse County Airport. Heavy snowfall accumulations over 6 inches were measured by area observers along with Interstate travel being closed on I-25 for crews to clear and keep folks off the road for safety.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the winter storm moved through the area, conditions along Interstate 80 on the afternoon of the 20th quickly deteriorated with icy conditions and limited visibility from blowing snow. A single vehicle rollover crash occurred at 4 PM on Wednesday where a female passenger who was not wearing her seatbelt succombed to her injuries at the scene after collapsing upon exiting the crashed vehicle. The driver of the vehicle was driving too fast for the conditions as noted in the Wyoming Highway Patrol report and lost control as the vehicle exited the interstate and sliding before rolling once. Across the majority of Carbon County, heavy snowfall amounts reaching up to six inches and drifts as high as two feet were reported along with the Old Battle SNOTEL in the Sierra Madre measuring twelve inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low moved across Wyoming during the 12th and 13th. This low provided a deep upslope flow across the northern Big Horn Mountains resulting in heavy snow, strong gusty winds, as well as blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across the area were in the 12 to 14 inch range. In addition, winds gusted to 45 mph. This resulted in blowing and drifting snow resulting in the closure of US 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low moved across Wyoming during the 12th and 13th. This low provided low-level moisture to advect into Sheridan County resulting in heavy snow. In addition, tight surface pressure gradients associated with the low resulted in very gusty winds. Blizzard conditions occurred across the area resulting in severe driving conditions and interstate closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 3 to 10 inches were reported across the area. Very gusty winds resulted in blowing snow and low visibilities. Interstate 90 south of Sheridan was closed for a period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moved over the top of a ridge and brought severe thunderstorms to portions of northern and central Wyoming. The strongest storm moved through Hot Springs County, where a supercell thunderstorm brought hail over two inches in diameter, including to the city of Thermopolis, Wyoming. There were numerous reports of broken windows on homes and cars as well as damaged siding in the city of Thermopolis.  Another storm brought quarter sized hail northwest of Riverton. Another thunderstorm rapidly developed over Natrona County, southwest of Casper and brought golf ball sized hail along Highway 220.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm developed over the Lee of the Absarokas and quickly became severe as it moved eastward into Hot Springs County. On a long path there were numerous reports of golf ball sized hail, with a maximum size of over two inches in diameter at Hot Springs State Park, just east of Thermopolis, Wyoming. There were numerous reports of damaged siding as well as broken windows on cars and buildings along the hail path.
